commonIssues: Overwatering and poorly drained soil cause root rot; oregano is drought-tolerant once established and prefers to dry out between waterings.

Marginally hardy in colder regions (true oregano is only marginally hardy in zone 5); apply a winter mulch of evergreen boughs or straw after the soil freezes and remove it as growth resumes in spring.
Best flavor develops in full sun; too much shade gives weak, leggy growth and reduced essential-oil content.
Container plants need a drainage hole and gritty, free-draining mix; standing water in pots is the main cause of failure.

source.citation: The Old Farmer's Almanac — Oregano: Planting, Growing, and Harvesting Oregano Plants. Oregano is a perennial in USDA Zones 4 to 9; loves full sun for strong flavor (partial shade in hot climates); harvest no more than one-third at a time, best flavor in midsummer before flowering.
The Old Farmer's Almanac — Oregano. Perennial in USDA Zones 4-9 (Zone 4 lower limit ~ -29 C); returns each year in well-draining soil that survives winter.

RHS — How to grow Oregano. Sun-loving Mediterranean herb needing full sun; drought-tolerant once established, container plants watered during dry spells but never waterlogged; ground plants in poor free-draining soil need no feeding.
RHS — How to grow Oregano. For a single young plant use a pot at least 15 cm (6 in) wide and deep, filled with soil-based compost mixed with up to 25% horticultural grit for drainage.
RHS — How to grow Oregano. Container plants benefit from a sprinkling of general-purpose granular feed in late spring or early summer after their first year; best planted late spring/early summer.

University of Illinois Extension — Oregano (Origanum vulgare var. hirtum). Requires a full sun location and good drainage for best growth and overwintering; grows 6 in to 2 ft tall; grows indoors in containers given at least 5-6 hours of sunlight per day; marginally hardy in zone 5; harvest before flowering leaving 4-6 pairs of leaves; winter mulch of evergreen boughs or straw after soil freezes.

University of Illinois Extension — Oregano. Good drainage required for best growth and overwintering; true oregano marginally hardy in zone 5, many desirable types treated as tender perennials needing winter protection.

Confidence note — baseIntervalDays (7) and hotWeatherMultiplier (0.5) are conservative estimates. Authoritative sources (RHS, Illinois Extension, Almanac) describe oregano qualitatively as drought-tolerant, watered only 'during dry spells', and preferring to dry out between waterings, but give no explicit numeric watering interval; 7 days is a conservative container default reflecting high drying tolerance, with reduced frequency-multiplier (water less) in hot established conditions per drought tolerance. feedingProfile baseIntervalDays (30) derived from the RHS 'once-a-year late-spring feed' guidance generalized to a monthly light feed for containers; ground plants typically need none.
